criteria: The agent has read Section 2.1 completely to understand the molecule graph representation G(V, E), the two template types (atom-template and bond-template), and the GNN split into feature extractor f and prediction head h.
The agent has read Algorithm 1 completely to understand the store construction procedure for atom store S_A and bond store S_B.
The agent has read Section 2.3 completely to understand the KNN retrieval equations (1-4) for combining GNN and KNN predictions.
The agent has read Section 2.4 completely to understand the adapter network architecture including the GIN layer, FC layers, and prediction of T_A, T_B, lambda_a, and lambda_b.
The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the dataset splits, preprocessing scripts, template counts, backbone GNN configuration, and evaluation protocol.
The agent has read Tables 1, 2, and 3 to understand the main results on USPTO-50K (with and without reaction type) and USPTO-MIT.
The agent has read Table 5 to understand the ablation study design with five system configurations.
The agent has read Table 6 to understand the study on the number of retrieved reactions (K in KNN) and its effect on accuracy.

The agent has read Section 4.2 to understand the zero-shot and few-shot experimental setup, including the filtering of reaction classes 6-10 for zero-shot and keeping 10% of classes 6-10 for few-shot.
The agent has read Table 7 to understand the datastore size statistics and inference latency measurements.
The plan includes training the backbone GNN (LocalRetro-style 6-layer MPNN + GRA) on USPTO-50K and USPTO-MIT datasets before building the KNN stores.
The plan includes building the atom store S_A and bond store S_B offline from the training data following Algorithm 1.
The plan includes implementing KNN retrieval using faiss with IndexIVFPQ index and K=32 neighbours.
The plan includes implementing the KNN probability computation for atom templates using Equation 1 with temperature T_A and indicator function.
The plan includes implementing the KNN probability computation for bond templates using Equation 2 with temperature T_B.
The plan includes implementing the GNN-KNN interpolation for atom and bond templates using interpolation factor lambda_a and lambda_b as in Equations 3 and 4.
The plan includes implementing the lightweight adapter network with one GIN layer followed by FC layers to predict T_A, T_B, lambda_a, and lambda_b adaptively per reaction.
The plan includes training the adapter on the validation set of USPTO-50K (10 epochs) and USPTO-MIT (2 epochs) with early stopping by best validation loss.
The plan includes running the ablation study with five system configurations: baseline, +KNN (fixed T and lambda), +KNN adaptive T, +KNN adaptive lambda, full RetroKNN.
The plan includes running zero-shot and few-shot experiments by filtering USPTO-50K reactions by class (classes 1-5 for training, classes 6-10 for evaluation).
The plan includes ranking all atom and bond template predictions together and outputting the top 50 candidates for each target molecule.
The plan includes computing top-K accuracy for K=1, 3, 5, 10, and 50 using the evaluation script from Chen and Jung (2021).
The code for loading and preprocessing USPTO-50K (40k/5k/5k split) using the preprocessing scripts from Chen and Jung (2021) to extract 658 reaction templates has been implemented.
The code for loading and preprocessing USPTO-MIT (409k/40k/30k split) using the preprocessing scripts from Chen and Jung (2021) to extract 20,221 reaction templates has been implemented.
The code for extracting atom and bond input features using DGL-LifeSci has been implemented.
The backbone GNN feature extractor f has been implemented as a 6-layer MPNN followed by a single GRA layer with 8 attention heads and hidden dimension 320.
The backbone GNN applies dropout with rate 0.2 during training.
The prediction head h has been implemented as two dense layers with ReLU activation to predict template probability distributions over T_a and T_b.
The backbone GNN training uses Adam optimizer with learning rate 0.001, runs for up to 50 epochs, and early stops when validation loss does not improve for 5 consecutive epochs.

The store construction (Algorithm 1) iterates all training molecules, extracts hidden representations h_v for each node and h_e for each edge using the trained feature extractor f, and stores (h_v, t) for reaction center atoms and (h_v, 0) for non-center atoms in S_A.
The store construction (Algorithm 1) stores (h_e, t) for reaction center bonds and (h_e, 0) for non-center bonds in S_B, producing atom store S_A and bond store S_B.
The KNN search is implemented using the faiss library with IndexIVFPQ index for fast approximate nearest neighbor search in both atom store S_A and bond store S_B.
The number of nearest neighbors K is set to 32 for the KNN search during inference.

The KNN atom template probability P_KNN(t_a | M, a) has been implemented as Equation 1: a sum over K neighbors where each neighbor's weight is exp(-d(h_a, h_i)/T_A) and only neighbors with matching template t_i = t_a contribute via the indicator function.
The KNN bond template probability P_KNN(t_b | M, b) has been implemented as Equation 2: a sum over K neighbors with weights exp(-d(h_b, h_i)/T_B) using the indicator function for template matching.
The combined atom template probability P(t_a | M, a) has been implemented as Equation 3: a linear interpolation lambda_a * P_GNN(t_a | M, a) + (1 - lambda_a) * P_KNN(t_a | M, a).
The combined bond template probability P(t_b | M, b) has been implemented as Equation 4: a linear interpolation lambda_b * P_GNN(t_b | M, b) + (1 - lambda_b) * P_KNN(t_b | M, b).

The adapter GIN layer for node features h_v^(g) has been implemented as Equation 5: h_v^(g) = W_vg * ((1 + epsilon) * h_v + sum_{e in E(v)} ReLU(h_v + h_e)) + b_vg, where epsilon and W_vg are learnable parameters.
The adapter FC projection of atom KNN distances h_v^(k) has been implemented as Equation 6: h_v^(k) = W_vk * {d(h_v, h_i)}_{i=1}^{K} + b_vk, building a K-dimensional vector of distances.
The adapter FC projection of bond KNN distances h_e^(k) has been implemented as Equation 7: h_e^(k) = W_ek * {d(h_e, h_i)}_{i=1}^{K} + b_ek, building a K-dimensional vector of distances.
The combined node representation h_v^(o) has been implemented as Equation 8: ReLU(W_vo * ReLU(h_v^(g) || h_v^(k)) + b_vo), where || denotes tensor concatenation.
The combined edge representation h_e^(o) has been implemented as Equation 9: ReLU(W_eo * ReLU(h_es^(g) || h_et^(g) || h_e^(k)) + b_eo), concatenating start-node, end-node, and KNN distance features.
The softmax temperature T_A for atom KNN is predicted from h_v^(o) and clamped to [1, 100] as in Equation 10: T_A = max(1, min(100, W_ta * h_v^(o) + b_ta)).
The interpolation factor lambda_a for atom templates is predicted from h_v^(o) using sigmoid as in Equation 11: lambda_a = sigma(W_la * h_v^(o) + b_la) to ensure lambda_a in (0, 1).
The softmax temperature T_B for bond KNN is predicted from h_e^(o) and clamped to [1, 100] as in Equation 12: T_B = max(1, min(100, W_tb * h_e^(o) + b_tb)).
The interpolation factor lambda_b for bond templates is predicted from h_e^(o) using sigmoid as in Equation 13: lambda_b = sigma(W_lb * h_e^(o) + b_lb) to ensure lambda_b in (0, 1).
The adapter training loss L_M has been implemented as Equation 14: the negative log-likelihood over both atom and bond template predictions, averaged over all atoms and bonds in the target molecule.
The adapter uses the same hidden dimension (320) as the backbone GNN and is trained with Adam optimizer at learning rate 0.001.
The adapter is trained for 10 epochs on USPTO-50K validation set and 2 epochs on USPTO-MIT validation set, selecting the model with best validation loss.
The inference procedure ranks all non-zero atom and bond template predictions by their combined probability and outputs the top 50 reactions as the final output.

The ablation study code for system 2 (KNN only without adapter) includes a grid search over T in {1, 5, 25, 50} and lambda in {0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 0.9} for a total of 20 combinations, selecting parameters by validation loss.
The code for zero-shot dataset creation filters USPTO-50K training and validation data to retain only reaction classes 1-5, while evaluating on classes 6-10.
The code for few-shot dataset creation retains only 10% of reactions with classes 6-10 in the training data while keeping all other classes intact.
The evaluation code computes top-K exact match accuracy for K=1, 3, 5, 10, and 50 using the evaluation script from LocalRetro (Chen and Jung 2021).
The backbone GNN has been trained successfully on the USPTO-50K dataset and saved for downstream store construction and adapter training.
The backbone GNN has been trained successfully on the USPTO-MIT dataset and saved for downstream store construction and adapter training.
The atom store S_A and bond store S_B have been constructed successfully from the USPTO-50K training data (|S_A|=1,039k, |S_B|=2,241k) and indexed with faiss.
The atom store S_A and bond store S_B have been constructed successfully from the USPTO-MIT training data (|S_A|=10,012k, |S_B|=21,495k) and indexed with faiss.
The adapter network has been trained successfully on the USPTO-50K validation set and the adapter with best validation loss is selected for testing.
The adapter network has been trained successfully on the USPTO-MIT validation set and the adapter with best validation loss is selected for testing.
The full RetroKNN inference has been executed successfully on the USPTO-50K test set without errors, producing top-50 candidate templates for each target molecule.
The full RetroKNN inference has been executed successfully on the USPTO-MIT test set without errors, producing top-50 candidate templates for each target molecule.
All five ablation system configurations (baseline, +KNN, +KNN adaptive T, +KNN adaptive lambda, full RetroKNN) have been executed successfully on USPTO-50K.
The K-size study experiments with K in {1, 4, 8, 16, 32} have been executed successfully, training a separate adapter for each K value.
The zero-shot experiments comparing LocalRetro baseline and RetroKNN across reaction classes 6-10 on the modified USPTO-50K have been executed successfully.
The few-shot experiments comparing LocalRetro baseline and RetroKNN across reaction classes 6-10 on the 10%-filtered USPTO-50K have been executed successfully.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 1: RetroKNN achieves top-1 accuracy of 57.2 on USPTO-50K (reaction type unknown), outperforming the LocalRetro backbone at 53.4.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 1: RetroKNN achieves top-3 accuracy 78.9, top-5 accuracy 86.4, top-10 accuracy 92.7, and top-50 accuracy 98.1 on USPTO-50K (reaction type unknown).
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: RetroKNN achieves top-1 accuracy of 66.7 on USPTO-50K when the reaction type is given, improving over LocalRetro at 63.9.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 3: RetroKNN achieves top-1 accuracy of 60.6 on USPTO-MIT, improving by 6.5 points over the LocalRetro backbone at 54.1.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 3: RetroKNN achieves top-3 accuracy 77.1, top-5 accuracy 82.3, top-10 accuracy 87.3, and top-50 accuracy 92.9 on USPTO-MIT.

The ablation study results match Table 5: system 1 (baseline) achieves 53.4, system 2 (+KNN fixed) achieves 56.3, system 3 (+KNN adaptive T) achieves 56.7, system 4 (+KNN adaptive lambda) achieves 56.8, and system 5 (full RetroKNN) achieves 57.2.
The K-size study results match Table 6: K=1 achieves 55.6, K=4 achieves 57.4, K=8 achieves 57.1, K=16 achieves 56.9, and K=32 achieves 57.2 top-1 accuracy on USPTO-50K.

The execution outcomes confirm the zero-shot study in Figure 4(a, c): RetroKNN achieves non-zero top-5 and top-10 accuracy on reaction classes 6-10 while the LocalRetro baseline achieves zero accuracy on most classes.

The execution outcomes confirm the few-shot study in Figure 4(b, d): RetroKNN consistently outperforms the LocalRetro baseline across all reaction classes 6-10 with an average improvement of 8.56 points top-5 and 5.64 points top-10 accuracy.
The execution outcomes confirm the datastore sizes in Table 7: the atom store S_A contains approximately 1,039k entries and bond store S_B contains approximately 2,241k entries for USPTO-50K.

The execution outcomes confirm the inference latency in Table 7: the average per-reaction latency with KNN retrieval is approximately 3.31 ms on USPTO-50K versus 2.71 ms without KNN, measured on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU.
